mnemonics: LISAM: Linear search Is Simple, Always More — Insertion, Searching, and Array complexities align with O(n) for most operations
SIX DS OPERATIONS: Search, Insert, Delete, Traverse, Sort, Merge — a simple way to recall the six fundamental operations
Binary Search: Half Each Time — B-H-E-T, remember that log₂n gives the maximum iterations
Merge Sort: Divide And Conquer Then Merge — D-A-C-T-M: split, sort halves, merge results

acronyms.expansion: Traversal, Insertion, Searching, Deletion, Sorting, Operations — six fundamental operations on Data Structures
acronyms.usage: Remember this acronym when asked to list basic data structure operations

acronyms.expansion: Best, Average, Worst case analysis for algorithm complexity
acronyms.usage: Used when analyzing performance of searching algorithms

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a balanced binary tree where each node splits the search space in half. Starting from root, go left if smaller, right if larger. Depth equals log₂n levels. For 1000 elements, only ~10 levels to search.
Array insertion at front: think of inserting a card at the beginning of a deck — must move all existing cards. Linked list: think of adding a link to a chain — just reconnect two ends.
Think of sorting playing cards: divide into two halves, sort each half (recursively), then merge by picking the smaller card from either half — this is exactly the merge operation.

analogies.analogy: Like searching for a book in an unsorted library shelf — you must check each book one by one until finding it or reaching the end.
Like searching for a name in a phone book (alphabetically sorted) — open to middle, decide which half, repeat. Much faster than checking every name.
Like a security guard walking through every room of a building exactly once to check all areas — must visit each room systematically.
Like merging two sorted decks of cards by repeatedly picking the smaller top card from either deck until both are empty.
